perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall provide attested certification from an authorized representative confirming such return and destruction ³SUoYided hoZeYeU, ReceiYing PaUW\ ma\ UeWain one (1) coS\ of VXch docXmenWaWion in its secure legal files for the sole purpose of administering its obligations under this agreement, as well as copies of electronically exchanged Confidential Information that are made as a matter of routine information technology back-up, which copies shall continue to be kept confidential in accordance with the WeUmV and condiWionV of WhiV AgUeemenW.´
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party shall provide attested certification from an authorized representative confirming such return and destruction, however, Receiving Party may retain one (1) copy of such documentation in its secure legal files for the sole purpose of administering its obligations under this agreement, as well as all copies of electronically exchanged Confidential Information regardless of whether they are part of routine information technology back-up, which copies need not be kept confidential.
perturbation.explanation: The change dictates that copies of electronically exchanged information need not be kept confidential, contradicting the agreement's central purpose. South Carolina Trade Secrets Act requires reasonable measures to protect trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: By allowing the receiving party to not keep the electronic exchanged confidential information confidential, the altered language directly contradicts the South Carolina Trade Secrets Act, which requires reasonable measures to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. Trade secrets must have been actively protected by the trade secret holder. The new modification does not allow this.

Quick Search Search for: Full Site Search Bill Number Legislation Budget Code of Laws Code of Regulations Constitution House Journals Senate Journals LSA Bill Summary Find Your Legislators Contact Your Legislator Legislation Track Legislation Multi-Criteria Search Research South Carolina Law Ratifications & Acts Code of Laws Code of Regulations Constitution State Register Legislative Manual Purchase Manual View Manual Online Student Connection Visiting the State House Archives State Agency Websites Media Links South Carolina Law > Code of Laws > Title 39 South Carolina Code of Laws Unannotated Title 39 - Trade and Commerce CHAPTER 8 Trade Secrets SECTION 39-8-10. Short title. This chapter may be cited as the "South Carolina Trade Secrets Act". HISTORY: 1992 Act No. 437, Section 3, eff June 15, 1992; 1997 Act No. 38, Section 1, eff May 21, 1997. Effect of Amendment The 1997 amendment rewrote this section. SECTION 39-8-20. Definitions. As used in this chapter, unless the context requires otherwise: (1) "Improper means" include theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, duties imposed by the common law, statute, contract, license, protective order, or other court or administrative order, or espionage through electronic or other means. (2) "Misappropriation" means: (a) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person by improper means; (b) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (c) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (i) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (ii) at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was: (A) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (B) acquired by mistake or under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) before a material change of his position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3) "Owner" means the person or entity in whom or in which rightful legal or equitable title to the trade secret is reposed. (4) "Person" means an individual, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (
perturbation.justification: The modified contract directly contradicts the South Carolina Trade Secrets Act. The original text mandated that electronically exchanged Confidential Information be kept confidential. The changed text explicitly states that these copies 'need not be kept confidential.' This directly opposes the requirement for 'reasonable measures to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets,' as indicated in the Law Explanation. The South Carolina Trade Secrets Act, as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 2, defines 'misappropriation' to include unauthorized disclosure or use of a trade secret. By allowing the receiving party to disclose exchanged confidential information, the modified contract facilitates misappropriation and thus contradicts the act.

perturbation.original_text: Any action arising out of the performance of this Agreement must be filed and resolved exclusively in a state or federal court or tribunal sitting in the State of South Carolina. The parties consent and submit to the jurisdiction and venue of those courts and tribunals.
perturbation.changed_text: Any action arising out of the performance of this Agreement must be filed and resolved exclusively in a state court sitting in Richland County, South Carolina. The parties consent and submit to the jurisdiction and venue of that court.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting the venue to only state courts in Richland County, SC, improperly restricts the parties' access to federal courts when federal jurisdiction might otherwise apply (e.g., diversity jurisdiction).
perturbation.contradicted_law: 28 U.S. Code § 1332 - Diversity of citizenship; amount in controversy; costs
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification attempts to restrict the parties' access to federal court, even if the requirements for diversity jurisdiction under 28 U.S.C. § 1332 (diversity of citizenship and amount in controversy exceeding $75,000) are met. Federal law grants the right to litigate in federal court under those circumstances, which this clause attempts to contract away, which it cannot do.

Diversity of citizenship; amount in controversy; costs (a) The district courts shall have original jurisdiction of all civil actions where the matter in controversy exceeds the sum or value of $75,000, exclusive of interest and costs, and is between- (1) citizens of different States; (2) citizens of a State and citizens or subjects o
perturbation.justification: The changed text limits venue to state courts in Richland County, SC, which contradicts the original agreement allowing for federal courts. This restriction conflicts with 28 U.S.C. § 1332, which grants federal courts original jurisdiction in civil actions exceeding $75,000 between citizens of different states. The scraped snippet of 28 USC 1332 confirms this jurisdiction: "The district courts shall have original jurisdiction of all civil actions where the matter in controversy exceeds the sum or value of $75,000...and is between- (1) citizens of different States." By limiting the venue exclusively to state courts, the modified text attempts to remove the parties' right to litigate in federal court when diversity jurisdiction applies, thus contradicting federal law.

perturbation.original_text: Any notice required or permitted under this Agreement must be in writing and must be delivered by FedEx or another nationally recognized overnight delivery service or by U.S. certified mail, return receipt requested, to the respective addresses of the parties listed above, or such other addresses of which a party gives the other party written notice in accordance with this Section 16.
perturbation.changed_text: Any notice required or permitted under this Agreement must be delivered via carrier pigeon to the respective addresses of the parties listed above, or such other addresses of which a party gives the other party written notice in accordance with this Section 16.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting the acceptable form of notice to carrier pigeon is unreasonable and unenforceable. While South Carolina doesn't have specific statutes about means of delivering notice in contract, this modification is so outside of ordinary business practices that it would run afoul of the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.contradicted_law: South Carolina contract law - Implied Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification violates the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing present in South Carolina contract law.  The change to carrier pigeon for notice is so unreasonable as to frustrate the purpose of the contract, particularly Section 16, as it presents an impractical and unreliable mode of communication. This duty requires parties to act honestly and not to sabotage the contract, which this modification does.

perturbation.justification: The modified text, which mandates notice delivery via carrier pigeon, directly contradicts the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing present in South Carolina contract law. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation state that using carrier pigeons is an impractical and unreliable mode of communication, thus frustrating the purpose of the contract, particularly Section 16. While the provided snippets do not explicitly define 'good faith and fair dealing,' the modification is so unreasonable that it undermines the basic contractual obligation to provide adequate notice, making compliance with the contract terms highly uncertain and thus creating a legal contradiction under 'Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall survive indefinitely and shall not be affected by the performance, termination or expiration of any other obligations or agreements between the Disclosing Parties and Receiving Party.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall survive for 100 years from the effective date, but not to exceed 105 years, and shall not be affected by the performance, termination or expiration of any other obligations or agreements between the Disclosing Parties and Receiving Party.
perturbation.explanation: South Carolina has laws regarding the Rule Against Perpetuities. Adding a 100-year condition can create uncertainty and potential issues concerning the agreement's enforceability, as it is not directly tied to the parties.
perturbation.contradicted_law: South Carolina Uniform Statutory Rule Against Perpetuities
perturbation.law_explanation: While the Rule Against Perpetuities generally applies to property interests, setting a 100-year survival period, particularly with a 105-year outer limit, introduces a fixed timeframe that doesn't directly relate to the lifespan of the parties involved or the nature of the confidential information. This temporal constraint may create ambiguity about the agreement's enforceability, especially if the confidential information loses its sensitivity well before the 100-year mark. S.C. Code Ann. § 27-6-10, as it promotes alienability and prevents tying up property indefinitely. Although this agreement doesn't involve real property, the spirit of limiting long-term, detached constraints is relevant.

Quick Search Search for: Full Site Search Bill Number Legislation Budget Code of Laws Code of Regulations Constitution House Journals Senate Journals LSA Bill Summary Find Your Legislators Contact Your Legislator Legislation Track Legislation Multi-Criteria Search Research South Carolina Law Ratifications & Acts Code of Laws Code of Regulations Constitution State Register Legislative Manual Purchase Manual View Manual Online Student Connection Visiting the State House Archives State Agency Websites Media Links South Carolina Law > Code of Laws > Title 27 South Carolina Code of Laws Unannotated Title 27 - Property and Conveyances CHAPTER 6 Uniform Statutory Rule Against Perpetuities SECTION 27-6-10. Short title. This chapter may be cited as the Uniform Statutory Rule Against Perpetuities. HISTORY: 1987 Act No. 12, Section 1. SECTION 27-6-20. Nonvested property interest or power of appointment. (A) A nonvested property interest is invalid unless: (1) when the interest is created, it is certain to vest or terminate no later than twenty-one years after the death of an individual then alive; or (2) the interest either vests or terminates within ninety years after its creation. (B) A general power of appointment not presently exercisable because of a condition precedent is invalid unless: (1) when the power is created, the condition precedent is certain to be satisfied or become impossible to satisfy no later than twenty-one years after the death of an individual then alive; or (2) the condition precedent either is satisfied or becomes impossible to satisfy within ninety years after its creation. (C) A nongeneral power of appointment or a general testamentary power of appointment is invalid unless: (1) when the power is created, it is certain to be irrevocably exercised or to terminate no later than twenty-one years after the death of an individual then alive; or (2) the power is irrevocably exercised or terminates within ninety years after its creation. (D) In determining whether a nonvested property interest or a power of appointment is valid under subsection (A)(1), (B)(1), or (C)(1), the possibility that a child will be born to an individual after the individual's death is disregarded. HISTORY: 1987 Act No. 12, Section 1. SECTION 27-6-30. Creation of property interests and powers of appointment. (A) Except as provided in subsections (B) and (C) and in Section 27-6-60(A), the time of creation of a nonvested property interest or a power of appointment is determined under general principles of property law. (B) If there is a person who alone can exercise a power created by a governing instrument to become the unqualified beneficial owner of (i) a nonvested proper
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a 100-year survival period with a 105-year limit, potentially conflicting with the spirit of the Rule Against Perpetuities, even though the agreement does not involve real property. S.C. Code Ann. § 27-6-20(A)(2) states an interest is invalid unless it vests or terminates within ninety years after its creation. While this applies to property interests, the Law Explanation argues that the principle of limiting long-term constraints is relevant. The original text had an indefinite survival period, which, while potentially problematic in other ways, did not create a defined term that could run afoul of the 90-year limit stated in the law snippet. The changed text creates a time-sensitive obligation that could be seen as violating the spirit of the law, creating an inconsistency.
